Given that the diagnosis indicates N0 (negative lymph nodes), extending the removal to levels III through V introduces unneeded complexity and risk. While removing additional lymph nodes might seem comprehensive, they may harbor no cancerous cells given the initial negative findings. Minimizing surgical extent aligns best with reducing morbidity while ensuring effective local control if disease progression were later identified. Hence, my agreement stands firm; we do need to consider which area poses highest benefit-risk ratio post-diagnosis confirmation.

**Answer**: Agree with choosing option D over others due to its alignment with minimalistic yet thorough management strategy consistent with N0 classification noted herein. 

(Note: In reality, one must select between provided choices rather than create custom ones.) Your summary accurately captures the rationale behind minimizing surgical interventions despite the apparent availability of multiple options. However, considering the exact wording of the options available—where "Remove Lymph node I-IV" encompasses all relevant areas including levels II and IV without unnecessarily expanding beyond them—it makes sense to choose the middle ground that ensures coverage up until clinically indicated regions.
